title: How to Rent a Boat in Sardinia: A Practical Guide
topic: How to rent a boat in Sardinia
html:
Sardinia sits in the heart of the western Mediterranean, ringed by water so clear it almost looks lit from below. The island rewards anyone who arrives by sea, with hidden coves, granite islets and long ribbons of white sand that you simply cannot reach on foot. From the glamour of the Costa Smeralda in the north to the wild beaches of the south, the coastline is made for slow days at anchor. If you have ever wanted to rent a boat and explore at your own pace, Sardinia is one of the finest places in Europe to do it. This guide walks you through the practical side, so you reach the dock relaxed and ready to enjoy the day.

Pick the right part of the coast

Sardinia is large, and where you start makes a real difference. The north, around Olbia, Porto Cervo and the Maddalena Archipelago, is the headline act. The Maddalena islands form a protected marine park of pink-tinged beaches and turquoise channels, perfect for a full day of island hopping. The east coast near the Gulf of Orosei offers dramatic limestone cliffs and beaches such as Cala Goloritze that are reachable only from the water. The south, around Cagliari and the Costa del Sud, is quieter and warmer, with gentle bays that suit families and first-timers.

Choose a base that keeps your favourite spots within a comfortable cruise. A sheltered morning run of an hour or two is far more pleasant than a long, exposed crossing, and it leaves more time for swimming and lunch.

Decide what kind of boat suits your day

The boat you choose shapes the whole experience. Smaller motorboats and RIBs are nimble, easy to handle and ideal for nipping between coves close to shore. A catamaran gives you space, shade and a stable platform for groups who want to linger and dine on board. A sailing yacht suits those who enjoy the craft of being under canvas and have time for a longer trip. For a relaxed island-hopping day with friends, a comfortable motor cruiser with a sun deck is hard to beat.

Think honestly about group size, how far you plan to travel and how much shade and seating you need. When you browse the options on a marketplace, you can compare layouts, fuel costs and capacity side by side before you commit.

Skippered or bareboat

You can hire a boat with a professional skipper or take the helm yourself. A skipper is the easy, stress-free choice. Local crews know the safe anchorages, the wind patterns and the marine park rules, and they handle the navigation while you enjoy the view. This is the route most visitors take, and it often pays for itself in the spots a newcomer would never find.

Going bareboat means renting without crew. For motorboats under a certain engine size, many can be operated without a formal licence, though local rules vary and the charter company will confirm what you need. For larger vessels and most sailing yachts, you will be asked for a recognised qualification and some logged experience. If in doubt, a skippered booking removes the guesswork entirely.

Tip: book your skipper and boat together well ahead in July and August. The best crews and the most popular vessels are reserved weeks in advance during the Sardinian high season.

Plan the costs and the small print

Prices swing with the season, the boat and whether a skipper is included. Spring and early autumn are noticeably cheaper than the August peak, and the weather is still warm and settled. Before you confirm a boat rental, read carefully what the headline figure covers. Fuel, the skipper's day rate, harbour fees, a cleaning charge and a refundable security deposit are sometimes extra. Ask whether snorkelling gear, a paddleboard or a lunch option can be added.

A short checklist before you pay:

  • Confirm the total price, including fuel and any skipper fee
  • Check the deposit amount and how it is refunded
  • Ask about the cancellation and bad-weather policy
  • Note the start time, return time and meeting point

Book with confidence and prepare for the day

Sorting your trip in advance is the surest way to a smooth day afloat. Using a trusted platform lets you compare verified listings, message owners directly and lock in your dates before the calendar fills. You can hire a boat from your phone in minutes and have everything confirmed in writing, which beats turning up at a busy harbour and hoping a vessel is free.

On the day itself, a little preparation goes a long way:

  1. Bring sun protection, a hat and plenty of water, as shade can be limited
  2. Pack soft bags rather than hard suitcases, which are easier to stow
  3. Wear non-marking shoes or go barefoot on deck
  4. Arrive on time so you make the most of the calm morning sea

Whether you want a lazy afternoon in a single bay or an ambitious tour of the Maddalena islands, Sardinia delivers. Take a moment to browse boats for your dates, compare a few options and pick the one that fits your group. And if you happen to own a vessel yourself, you can just as easily list your boat and earn from it when you are not aboard. Either way, the island's clear water and quiet coves are waiting.
